options.d: Set up VR stations at individual desks and have students use seated-only VR experiences

consequences.a: Without separate Guardian zones, multiple students in the same area risk bumping into each other and furniture. Free turns without structure lead to chaos and wasted time.
consequences.b: Excellent! Safety first with Guardian boundaries in defined zones, plus organized rotations ensure every student gets hands-on time within the class period.
consequences.c: Moving desks creates space, but 5 students in VR at once in the same open area risks collisions — they can't see each other! Without defined Guardian zones and a rotation plan, this is a safety hazard.
consequences.d: Seated VR at desks is safe and manageable, but it limits students to only certain apps and misses the immersive room-scale experiences. Good as a fallback, but not the optimal classroom setup.

storeInstall.prompt: Before class, install the apps you need from the Meta Quest Store and verify they’re ready.

items.1: Open the Store from the headset menu or the Meta Quest app
items.2: Search the app by name (or browse categories)
items.4: Wait for download and installation to finish
items.5: Confirm the app appears in the library

casting.scenario: You need 25 students to see what one student is experiencing in VR. Your classroom has a projector connected to Wi-Fi and the Meta Quest app installed on your phone.

options.a: Have each student try the headset one by one while the rest watch from behind
options.b: Cast the headset view to the classroom projector using the Meta Quest app
options.c: Connect the Quest 3 to the projector with an HDMI cable
options.d: Take screenshots inside VR and email them to students after class

consequences.a: This takes too long — with 25 students and one headset, each person barely gets a minute. The rest of the class is idle and loses interest. Casting to a shared screen is far more efficient.
consequences.b: Excellent choice! Casting mirrors the VR view in real time on the projector, so the whole class follows along. You can narrate, pause, and discuss what everyone sees. This is the recommended approach for group demonstrations.
consequences.c: The Quest 3 doesn’t have an HDMI port! It only has USB-C. For wireless display, use the casting feature through the Meta Quest app or a Chromecast-compatible device.
consequences.d: Screenshots miss the immersive, real-time aspect of VR. Students can’t see movement, spatial relationships, or interact with the moment. Casting is live and much more engaging for the classroom.

preClassChecklist.prompt: Complete this readiness check before students arrive. Go through each item and verify it’s done.

items.1: All headsets charged to at least 80%
items.2: Wi-Fi connected and working on every headset
items.3: Guardian boundaries configured in each VR zone
items.4: Target apps installed, updated, and tested
items.5: Head straps adjusted to fit various sizes
items.6: Casting tested on projector or external screen
items.7: Rotation groups planned and written on the board

firstSetup.title: First Setup
firstSetup.description: Unbox and prepare a Quest 3 headset for the first time.

s1.context: You've just received brand new Quest 3 headsets for your classroom. Before anyone puts them on, there are some important setup steps.
s1.body: A proper first setup ensures clear visuals, comfort, and a smooth first experience for students. Let's walk through it step by step.

s2.context: You pick up the first headset. The LED indicator is off and you're not sure about the battery level.
s2.tip: The LED color tells you the battery status: orange means charging, solid white means powered on.

choices.a: Power on the headset immediately and start using it
choices.b: Connect the USB-C charger and check the LED indicator
choices.c: Ask a student to figure out the battery situation

feedback.a: Powering on without checking the battery risks the headset dying mid-session. Always check charge first!
feedback.b: Correct! Checking the LED tells you if it needs charging. Orange means it's charging — wait for white before using.
feedback.c: It's better to handle setup yourself so you know the exact state of each headset before class.

s3.context: The headset is charged. Now you need to configure the language so students see the interface in the right language.

choices.a: Skip it — the default English is fine for everyone
choices.b: Go to Settings > System > Language and select the language your students use
choices.c: Use the Meta Quest phone app to change the language remotely

feedback.a: If your students speak a different language, they'll struggle to navigate. Always match the interface language to your class.
feedback.b: Perfect! Setting the correct language from the headset ensures students can navigate menus and understand prompts.
feedback.c: The phone app works, but it's faster to do it directly on the headset during initial setup.

s4.context: Time to adjust the IPD (interpupillary distance). A student will use this headset next period.
s4.tip: IPD affects image clarity. Incorrect IPD causes blurry or double vision and eye strain.

choices.a: Skip IPD — students can adjust it themselves when they notice blur
choices.b: Use the IPD wheel to set a neutral middle position as a starting point
choices.c: Set it to the widest position for maximum field of view

feedback.a: Students often don't know what IPD is or how to fix blur. Set a good default so the first experience is clear.
feedback.b: Smart! A neutral middle position works for most people. You can fine-tune it for individual students during the session.
feedback.c: The widest setting only works for people with wide-set eyes. Most students would see a blurry, distorted image.

s5.context: The headset is almost ready. Last step: fitting the head strap properly.

choices.a: Tighten the strap as much as possible so the headset doesn't move
choices.b: Adjust it to be snug but comfortable, with weight evenly distributed
choices.c: Leave it loose so students can quickly take it on and off

feedback.a: Over-tightening causes discomfort, pressure marks, and headaches — especially during longer sessions.
feedback.b: Correct! A snug fit with even weight distribution keeps the image clear and prevents discomfort.
feedback.c: A loose strap means the headset shifts during movement, causing blur and possible drops.

s6.context: You've completed the first setup. Time for a final check before handing it to students.
s6.confirm: I've verified: display is clear, language is correct, strap fits comfortably, and battery is sufficient

classroomSetup.title: Classroom Setup
classroomSetup.description: Prepare your classroom space and headsets for a VR session.

s1.context: It's 15 minutes before class. You have 5 Quest 3 headsets for 25 students.
s1.body: A well-organized classroom setup prevents accidents, saves time, and ensures every student gets a turn. Let's prepare the space.

s2.context: You need to arrange the room. Currently, desks are in rows with narrow aisles.

choices.a: Keep the desks as they are — students will be seated during VR
choices.b: Create 5 designated VR zones with at least 2m between them, moving desks aside
choices.c: Push all furniture against the walls to create one large open space

feedback.a: Seated VR works for some apps, but many require standing or room-scale. Desks in rows limit movement and risk bumps.
feedback.b: Excellent! Separate zones with clear boundaries prevent collisions and give each student safe space to move.
feedback.c: One big open area sounds good but makes it hard to manage Guardian boundaries and students may wander into each other.

s3.context: The room is set up. Now you need to connect all headsets to WiFi.
s3.tip: VR apps download large files. A strong, stable WiFi connection prevents interruptions mid-experience.

choices.a: Hope the school WiFi is working — it usually is
choices.b: Pre-connect each headset to WiFi and test signal strength in each VR zone
choices.c: Use your phone's mobile hotspot for all 5 headsets

feedback.a: Never assume! School WiFi can be unreliable, especially with 5 headsets streaming simultaneously.
feedback.b: Perfect! Pre-testing WiFi in each zone catches dead spots before students encounter them.
feedback.c: A phone hotspot can't reliably handle 5 VR headsets. Bandwidth and latency will cause problems.

s4.context: WiFi is confirmed. Time to set up Guardian boundaries in each zone.

choices.a: Skip Guardian — the zones are clearly marked with tape on the floor
choices.b: Draw a Guardian boundary in each designated VR zone
choices.c: Set one large Guardian boundary covering the whole room

feedback.a: Tape on the floor is invisible inside VR! Guardian is the only safety boundary students can actually see.
feedback.b: Correct! Individual Guardian boundaries per zone keep each student safely within their space.
feedback.c: A room-wide boundary defeats the purpose — students won't get warnings before entering someone else's zone.

s5.context: Boundaries are set. You have 25 students and 5 headsets. How do you organize rotations?

choices.a: Number each headset 1-5 and assign 5 groups of 5 students with a rotation schedule on the board
choices.b: Place all headsets on one table and let students come up when they're ready
choices.c: Let students form their own groups and figure out turns

feedback.a: Smart! Clear groups and a visible schedule means no confusion, no arguments, and guaranteed equal time for everyone.
feedback.b: This creates a crowd around one table and leads to pushing, unfair turns, and wasted transition time.
feedback.c: Self-organized rotations almost always result in some students dominating time while quieter ones miss out.

s6.context: Almost ready. Should you do a final test on one headset before students arrive?

choices.a: Just check that it turns on — if it boots, it's fine
choices.b: Launch the target app, test Guardian triggers, verify casting to the display, and time the experience
choices.c: No need to test — you tested last week and nothing changed

feedback.a: Booting up doesn't confirm the app works, WiFi is connected, or casting functions properly.
feedback.b: Thorough! Testing the full flow catches issues before 25 students are watching. Always test the day-of.
feedback.c: Updates, WiFi changes, and app patches happen constantly. Last week's test tells you nothing about today.

installApps.title: Install Apps
installApps.description: Find, install, and verify educational VR apps on all headsets.

s1.context: You need to install an educational VR app called 'Wander' on all 5 headsets before tomorrow's geography lesson.
s1.body: Installing apps ahead of time prevents delays during class. Let's walk through finding, installing, and verifying the app.

s2.context: Where do you look for the app?

choices.a: Search Google on your phone for the app download link
choices.b: Open the Meta Quest Store from within the headset and search 'Wander'
choices.c: Ask students to find and install it on their own phones

feedback.a: Google can point you to the store link, but you still need to install from within the headset itself.
feedback.b: Correct! The Meta Quest Store is the official source. Search directly from the headset for the fastest install.
feedback.c: Students can't install apps on your classroom headsets from their phones — the headset's own account is needed.

s3.context: You search for 'Wander' but it doesn't appear in the store results.
s3.tip: Some apps are on App Lab (Meta's early access platform) and don't show in the main store search.

choices.a: Give up and look for a different geography app
choices.b: Check App Lab, try the direct URL link, or search via the Meta Quest phone app
choices.c: Assume the app was removed from the store

feedback.a: Don't give up too fast! Many educational apps are on App Lab, which requires a direct link or specific search.
feedback.b: Smart! Many great educational VR apps live on App Lab. The direct link or phone app search usually finds them.
feedback.c: Store search can be unreliable. Always try App Lab and the phone app before assuming an app is gone.

s4.context: The app installs on 4 headsets, but the 5th shows 'Not enough storage space'.

choices.a: Skip this headset — 4 is enough
choices.b: Go to Settings > Storage, uninstall unused apps to free space, then retry the install
choices.c: Factory reset the headset to clear everything and start fresh

feedback.a: With 25 students and rotations, every headset counts. Fixing the storage issue is worth the 5 minutes.
feedback.b: Correct! Removing unused apps is quick and targeted. Check which apps take the most space first.
feedback.c: A factory reset clears EVERYTHING — WiFi, Guardian, all apps. Way too drastic for a simple storage issue.

s5.context: All 5 headsets now have the app installed. Time for a final verification.
s5.confirm: I've verified: the app appears in the library on every headset, launches correctly, and is the latest version

castShare.title: Cast & Share
castShare.description: Set up casting so the whole class can see what's happening in VR.

s1.context: You want the whole class (25 students) to see what one student experiences inside VR, projected on the classroom display.
s1.body: Casting lets non-VR students follow along, participate in discussions, and learn from the VR user's experience. Let's set it up.

s2.context: How will you share the headset's view with the class?

choices.a: Pass the headset around so everyone can look inside
choices.b: Cast wirelessly from the headset to the classroom projector using the Meta Quest app
choices.c: Connect an HDMI cable from the headset to the projector
choices.d: Screen-record the VR session and play it back later

feedback.a: Passing headsets is slow, unhygienic, and means only one student sees at a time. Not scalable for 25 students.
feedback.b: Perfect! Wireless casting via the Meta Quest app shows the VR view on any screen. The whole class watches in real-time.
feedback.c: Quest 3 has no HDMI port. It only supports wireless casting or USB-C to a compatible PC.
feedback.d: Playback works but kills the real-time engagement. Students can't react, discuss, or guide the VR user live.

s3.context: Casting is working but there's a 3-second delay and the projected image is choppy.
s3.tip: Casting quality depends heavily on WiFi. The headset and display should be on the same network with minimal interference.

choices.a: Tell students to ignore the lag — the VR user has no delay
choices.b: Move the WiFi router closer, reduce other network traffic, and ensure both devices are on the same 5GHz band
choices.c: Switch to a wired USB-C connection to the projector

feedback.a: Lag makes it impossible for observers to follow along. The shared experience IS the teaching moment.
feedback.b: Correct! Casting lag is almost always a WiFi issue. 5GHz band, proximity, and reduced traffic solve most problems.
feedback.c: USB-C casting requires a compatible PC as a middleman, not a direct projector connection. It's more complex than fixing WiFi.

s4.context: The video is smooth now. But the class can't hear the VR app's audio from the projected view.

choices.a: Tell the VR user to describe what they hear out loud
choices.b: Connect external speakers to the casting display, or use a Bluetooth speaker paired to the headset
choices.c: Have students put in their own earbuds

feedback.a: Having the VR user narrate breaks their immersion and is unreliable. Audio should be part of the shared experience.
feedback.b: Correct! External speakers connected to the display or Bluetooth give the whole room clear audio alongside the video.
feedback.c: Individual earbuds can't receive the VR headset's audio. Only the casting display or paired speakers can.

s5.context: Casting is fully set up: video is smooth and audio is clear.
s5.confirm: I've verified: casting shows smooth video on the projector, audio is audible to the whole room, and there's minimal delay

preClassCheck.title: Pre-Class Checklist
preClassCheck.description: Final readiness check before students arrive.

s1.context: Students arrive in 10 minutes. Time for a final readiness check of all equipment and setup.
s1.body: A quick systematic check catches last-minute issues. Better to discover a dead battery now than when a student is waiting.

s2.context: Check that all headsets are charged to at least 80%.
s2.confirm: All headsets verified at 80%+ battery (LED shows solid white when powered on)

s3.context: Confirm WiFi connectivity on every headset.
s3.confirm: WiFi connected and tested on every headset — pages load, apps can reach servers

s4.context: Verify Guardian boundaries are properly configured in each VR zone.
s4.confirm: Guardian boundaries set in all zones — visual warnings trigger when approaching the edge

s5.context: Verify the target app is ready on all headsets.
s5.confirm: Target app installed, launched and tested on every headset — casting verified to the display

s6.context: During your final check, one headset won't turn on. You press the power button — nothing happens.
s6.tip: A non-responsive headset might have a completely drained battery. A long press (10+ seconds) forces a reboot.

choices.a: Set it aside and run the class with one fewer headset
choices.b: Try a long-press power reset, plug in the charger, and if it still fails, redistribute your rotation groups
choices.c: Cancel the VR portion of today's lesson entirely

feedback.a: That works, but a long-press reset often fixes the issue in 30 seconds. It's worth trying before giving up.
feedback.b: Perfect approach! Long-press reset is the first thing to try. If it's truly dead, adjusting rotation groups keeps the lesson on track.
feedback.c: One broken headset out of five is no reason to cancel. Adapt the rotation — students barely notice the difference.

fullPrep.title: Boss Level: Full VR Class Prep
fullPrep.description: Combine everything you've learned. Prepare your first VR class from scratch.

s1.context: Tomorrow is your very first VR class ever. You have 8 Quest 3 headsets, 32 students, and one 50-minute period.
s1.body: This is the real deal. You'll walk through the complete preparation — from the evening before to post-session. Every decision counts!

s2.context: It's the evening before. You have 3 hours. What's your first priority?

choices.a: Charge all 8 headsets overnight, install and test the app on each one
choices.b: Create your lesson plan and figure out what app to use
choices.c: Watch YouTube tutorials about VR teaching until you feel confident

feedback.a: Correct! Hardware prep comes first — charging takes hours and you need to verify apps. The lesson plan should already exist at this point.
feedback.b: The lesson plan should have been done days ago. The evening before is for equipment preparation, not curriculum design.
feedback.c: Watching videos is research, not preparation. Your headsets need to be charged and apps installed tonight!

s3.context: Morning of. You arrive 30 minutes before class. How do you set up the room?

choices.a: Create 8 designated VR stations with marked boundaries, clear obstacles, and a rotation schedule on the board
choices.b: Push desks to the sides and create one big open VR space
choices.c: Leave desks as-is — students will use VR seated at their desks

feedback.a: Perfect! Designated stations with clear boundaries and a visible rotation plan keeps 32 students organized and safe.
feedback.b: One big space with 8 students in VR simultaneously is a recipe for collisions. Individual zones are much safer.
feedback.c: For your first class, standing VR gives students the best 'wow' experience. Seated works but limits the impact.

s4.context: Stations are set up. Now you need to configure WiFi and Guardian on all 8 headsets.
s4.tip: With 8 headsets on WiFi simultaneously, network load is significant. Test that the connection holds under full load.

choices.a: Connect all headsets to the 5GHz school WiFi, set Guardian per station, and run one full test with all 8 headsets active at once
choices.b: Connect to WiFi and set Guardian on just 2 headsets — if those work, the rest will too
choices.c: Skip WiFi for now — most VR apps work offline

feedback.a: Correct! Testing all 8 simultaneously catches bandwidth issues you'd miss with fewer headsets. Guardian per station is essential.
feedback.b: 2 headsets working doesn't mean 8 will. WiFi bandwidth gets shared — always test at full load.
feedback.c: Many educational VR apps need WiFi for content streaming, updates, and casting. Never assume offline works.

s5.context: While testing, one headset shows 'App update required' and the update is 1.5 GB. Class starts in 20 minutes.

choices.a: Start the update and hope it finishes in time
choices.b: Skip the update, try launching the current version. If it fails, use that headset with a backup app instead
choices.c: Remove this headset from rotation and run with 7

feedback.a: A 1.5 GB update on school WiFi might take 20+ minutes. Too risky with a hard deadline.
feedback.b: Smart! Older versions often still work. If not, having a backup app ready shows good contingency planning.
feedback.c: Dropping a headset should be the last resort. Try the workaround first — it takes 2 minutes to check.

s6.context: Students are arriving. How do you brief them about VR before they put on headsets?

choices.a: Hand out headsets immediately — students learn by doing
choices.b: Give a 3-minute safety briefing: Guardian boundaries, how to signal if dizzy, hygiene rules, and rotation schedule
choices.c: Show a 15-minute VR safety video before starting

feedback.a: Without a briefing, students won't know about Guardian, dizzy protocols, or rotations. Chaos is guaranteed.
feedback.b: Perfect! A short, focused briefing covers essentials without eating into VR time. 3 minutes saves 15 minutes of confusion.
feedback.c: 15 minutes of a 50-minute period is too much. Students will zone out. Keep the briefing short and memorable.

s7.context: Mid-session: a student takes off the headset looking pale and says they feel dizzy.

choices.a: Tell them to push through it — VR sickness passes quickly
choices.b: Have them sit down immediately, remove the headset, focus on a distant real-world object, and sip water
choices.c: Give the headset to another student so the session keeps moving

feedback.a: NEVER push through VR sickness. It can escalate to nausea and vomiting. Student safety comes first, always.
feedback.b: Correct! Standard VR sickness protocol: stop, sit, real-world focus point, hydrate. Check in after 5 minutes before any return to VR.
feedback.c: Address the student's wellbeing before worrying about headset utilization. Redistribute after they're comfortable.

s8.context: Half the class is waiting for their VR turn. They're getting bored. How do you engage the non-VR students?

choices.a: Cast one headset to the projector so the whole class watches, and give observers a worksheet with discussion questions
choices.b: Tell them to work on homework from other classes
choices.c: Let them use their phones while they wait

feedback.a: Excellent! Casting creates a shared experience. The worksheet turns observers into active participants analyzing the VR content.
feedback.b: This wastes the VR lesson's potential. Observers should be engaged with the VR content, not doing unrelated work.
feedback.c: Phones create distraction and disconnect. The class should feel like one shared learning experience, not fragmented time.

s9.context: The period is ending. How do you wrap up the VR session?

choices.a: Tell students to leave the headsets on their desks and go to next class
choices.b: Immediately start collecting headsets while students are still using them
choices.c: Give a 5-minute warning, then have each group power off and place their headset in the charging station while you lead a 2-minute debrief

feedback.a: Headsets left on desks drain battery, risk damage, and miss the chance for a reflective debrief.
feedback.b: Abruptly ending VR is jarring and disrespectful. Always give a warning so students can finish their experience.
feedback.c: Perfect wrap-up! The warning gives closure, students learn to care for equipment, and the debrief reinforces learning.

objectivesAudit.title: Objectives Audit
objectivesAudit.description: Evaluate whether learning objectives are suitable for VR.

s1.context: Your school is launching a VR program. Several departments have submitted learning objectives they want to achieve using VR headsets.
s1.body: Your job is to review each objective and decide whether VR is the right tool. Remember: VR works best when students DO — practice procedures, make decisions, respond to situations.

s2.context: Objective from the History department: 'Students will memorise key dates of the French Revolution.'

choices.a: Approve — VR immersion helps memorisation
choices.b: Reject — memorisation doesn't require immersion. Spaced repetition tools are more effective and cheaper.
choices.c: Approve with changes — add a VR timeline experience

feedback.a: Memorising dates is pure recall — it doesn't benefit from immersion. Flashcards with spaced repetition are proven to be faster and cheaper for this type of learning.
feedback.b: Correct. Memorisation is not an action — it's recall. VR adds no value to rote learning. Suggest the department use spaced repetition tools instead.
feedback.c: A timeline experience would be visually interesting but still passive. If the goal is memorisation, VR isn't the efficient choice.

s3.context: Objective from the Nursing department: 'Students will perform a primary patient assessment on a virtual trauma patient, identifying all life-threatening conditions within four minutes.'
s3.tip: Look for action verbs, specific context, and measurable criteria.

choices.a: Approve — this has an action verb (perform), specific context (virtual trauma patient), and criteria (all conditions, four minutes). Perfect for VR.
choices.b: Reject — patient assessment should only be done on real patients
choices.c: Approve but remove the time limit

feedback.a: Excellent evaluation. This objective has all three components: action (perform), context (virtual trauma patient), and criteria (identify all conditions within four minutes). VR is the ideal tool for safe, repeatable practice.
feedback.b: Real patients are the end goal, but you can't practice on real trauma patients safely. VR provides unlimited safe repetition before students ever touch a real patient.
feedback.c: The time limit is intentional — it simulates the real pressure of trauma assessment. Removing it weakens the realism that makes VR valuable.

s4.context: Objective from the English department: 'Students will appreciate the beauty of Shakespeare's language by watching a VR performance of Hamlet.'

choices.a: Approve — watching Shakespeare in VR is culturally enriching
choices.b: Reject — English literature has nothing to do with VR
choices.c: Reject — 'appreciate' is passive and unmeasurable. If rewritten to include active tasks (e.g., 'students will identify three dramatic techniques and explain their impact'), VR could work.

feedback.a: Culturally enriching, but passive. 'Appreciate' cannot be observed or measured. A projector would deliver the same viewing experience more comfortably. VR needs an active task.
feedback.b: VR can work for literature! Imagine students interacting with characters, making plot decisions, or staging scenes. The issue is the passive objective, not the subject.
feedback.c: Correct. The problem is 'appreciate' — it's passive and unmeasurable. With active tasks (identify techniques, make staging decisions, interact with characters), VR could add genuine value to literature study.

s5.context: Objective from the Safety department: 'Students will execute the complete fire evacuation protocol in a simulated office building, correctly guiding all occupants to designated assembly points within six minutes.'

choices.a: Approve — action verb (execute), specific context (simulated office), measurable criteria (all occupants, assembly points, six minutes). This is exactly what VR was made for.
choices.b: Reject — fire drills should be done in real buildings
choices.c: Approve but simplify it — too many criteria

feedback.a: Perfect evaluation. This objective leverages VR's core strengths: safe practice of a dangerous scenario, unlimited repetition, realistic pressure, and measurable outcomes. This is a model VR objective.
feedback.b: Real drills are important, but you can't safely simulate a real fire. VR lets students practice the complete protocol — including the stress and decision-making — before the real event.
feedback.c: Multiple criteria reflect the complexity of the real task. Fire evacuation requires getting everything right — timing, routes, accountability. Simplifying the objective would misrepresent the skill.

s6.context: You've reviewed all the objectives. Time to summarise your recommendations.
s6.confirm: I've evaluated each objective against VR suitability criteria: action verbs, specific context, measurable criteria, and the need for immersive practice.

writeObjectives.title: Write Strong Objectives
writeObjectives.description: Practice rewriting weak objectives into strong VR-ready ones.

s1.context: You've been asked to help three departments rewrite their VR learning objectives. Each objective needs improvement.
s1.body: Remember the formula: [Action verb] + [what] + [in what context] + [to what standard]. Strong VR objectives describe what students DO, not what they 'understand.'

s2.context: Original objective: 'Students will understand the importance of hand hygiene in healthcare settings.'
s2.tip: Replace the passive verb with an observable action. Add context and criteria.

choices.a: Students will understand hand hygiene better after using VR
choices.b: Students will perform the WHO five-step hand hygiene procedure on a virtual patient in a hospital simulation, completing all steps in the correct order without contamination.
choices.c: Students will watch a VR video about hand hygiene

feedback.a: This is the same passive objective with 'after using VR' appended. 'Understand better' is still unmeasurable. The verb needs to change.
feedback.b: Excellent rewrite! 'Perform' is an observable action. 'WHO five-step procedure on a virtual patient' is the context. 'All steps in correct order without contamination' is the criterion. This fully leverages VR.
feedback.c: Watching a video is passive — the verb is still 'watch,' not an action verb. VR's value is in doing, not viewing.

s3.context: Original objective: 'Students will learn about earthquake safety procedures.'

choices.a: Students will learn about earthquake safety using VR headsets
choices.b: Students will be aware of the importance of earthquake preparedness
choices.c: Students will execute the drop-cover-hold procedure during a simulated earthquake in a virtual classroom, reaching safety within 15 seconds of the first tremor.

feedback.a: Adding 'using VR headsets' doesn't fix the passive verb. 'Learn about' is still unmeasurable. The action needs to change.
feedback.b: 'Be aware' is even vaguer than 'learn about.' This goes backwards — from unmeasurable to even more unmeasurable.
feedback.c: Perfect! 'Execute' is an action verb. 'Simulated earthquake in a virtual classroom' is the context. 'Within 15 seconds' is the criterion. This is specific, measurable, and perfectly suited for VR.

s4.context: Original objective: 'Students will be familiar with laboratory safety equipment.'

choices.a: Students will identify, locate, and demonstrate the correct use of four safety devices (fire extinguisher, eye wash station, fume hood, emergency shower) in a virtual laboratory within 3 minutes.
choices.b: Students will become more familiar with lab safety equipment through VR
choices.c: Students will appreciate the importance of laboratory safety

feedback.a: Outstanding. Three action verbs (identify, locate, demonstrate), specific context (four safety devices in a virtual lab), and a clear criterion (correct use within 3 minutes). This is a model objective.
feedback.b: 'Become more familiar' is still passive and unmeasurable. How would you know if a student is 'more familiar'? The objective needs observable actions.
feedback.c: 'Appreciate' is passive and unmeasurable — a classic trap verb. Rewrite with action verbs like 'identify,' 'locate,' or 'demonstrate.'

s5.context: You've rewritten three objectives. Time to verify your understanding.
s5.confirm: I can now write VR objectives using the formula: Action verb + what + context + criteria. I avoid passive verbs like 'understand,' 'learn about,' and 'appreciate.'

passiveToActive.title: Passive to Active
passiveToActive.description: Convert passive VR activities into active learning experiences.

s1.context: Several teachers at your school are using VR, but most activities are passive — students watch and observe but don't interact.
s1.body: Your mission is to help each teacher transform their passive VR activity into an active one. Remember: the shift from 'seeing' to 'doing' is what makes VR truly effective.

s2.context: History teacher: 'Students watch a 360° tour of ancient Egyptian pyramids for 10 minutes.'

choices.a: This is already active — VR headsets make everything interactive
choices.b: Add a task: 'Students explore the pyramid and identify three engineering techniques used in construction, then explain how each technique solved a specific building challenge.'
choices.c: Replace VR with a textbook — pyramids don't need VR

feedback.a: Wearing a headset doesn't make an activity active. Looking around is still passive observation. Active learning requires a task — something to find, decide, or create.
feedback.b: Correct! Now students have a purpose (identify techniques), a task (explain how each solved a challenge), and a reason to engage actively with the environment. They're investigating, not just sightseeing.
feedback.c: VR can bring ancient structures to life in ways a textbook can't. The issue isn't VR — it's the lack of an active task.

s3.context: Science teacher: 'Students view a fly-through animation of the human circulatory system inside VR.'
s3.tip: Think about what task would turn observation into interaction.

choices.a: Transform it: 'Students trace the path of a blood cell through the circulatory system, making decisions at each junction (Which chamber? Which valve?) and diagnosing a patient with a blocked artery.'
choices.b: Add more fly-through videos of other body systems
choices.c: It's fine as is — the visuals are impressive enough

feedback.a: Excellent transformation. Students go from passengers on a pre-recorded tour to active navigators making decisions. Following a blood cell with choices at each junction is discovery-based learning at its best.
feedback.b: More passive content doesn't become active through volume. One interactive experience produces more learning than ten passive fly-throughs.
feedback.c: Impressive visuals engage students momentarily, but they retain very little from passive viewing (~10%). Active practice boosts retention to 75%. The visuals are the starting point, not the destination.

s4.context: Safety trainer: 'Workers watch a 360° video of a warehouse showing various hazards.'

choices.a: Add more 360° videos from different angles
choices.b: Replace VR with a PowerPoint presentation of hazard photos
choices.c: Transform it: 'Workers walk through the virtual warehouse and physically tag each hazard they spot, categorise it by risk level, and propose a corrective action — with a timer adding realistic pressure.'

feedback.a: More angles of passive viewing is still passive viewing. The missing piece is a task that requires workers to actively identify, categorise, and respond to hazards.
feedback.b: PowerPoint removes the immersion entirely. The goal is to add interactivity to the VR experience, not to abandon VR.
feedback.c: Perfect transformation. Workers go from passive viewers to active investigators — finding hazards, categorising them, and proposing solutions under time pressure. This is exactly how VR should be used for safety training.

s5.context: You've converted three passive activities into active ones.
s5.confirm: I understand the difference between seeing (passive observation) and doing (active interaction), and I can transform passive VR activities into active learning experiences.

sessionPlanning.title: Session Planning
sessionPlanning.description: Plan a complete VR learning session with briefing, experience, and debriefing.

s1.context: You're planning a VR session for a group of trainee paramedics. The topic is initial patient assessment at an accident scene.
s1.body: A complete VR session has three phases: briefing (under 60 seconds), active experience (5–15 minutes), and debriefing (5–10 minutes). Let's design each phase.

s2.context: Phase 1: Briefing. You have 60 seconds before students put on headsets. What should you say?
s2.tip: A good briefing covers four things: objective, task, time limit, and what success looks like.

choices.a: Just say 'Put on your headsets and explore the accident scene'
choices.b: 'Your task: perform a primary assessment on the virtual patient at the accident scene. You have 8 minutes. Success means identifying all life-threatening conditions and calling for the correct resources.'
choices.c: Give a 10-minute lecture on patient assessment theory before they start

feedback.a: 'Explore' gives no direction. Students won't know what to focus on and will waste time wandering. Briefing must state the specific task and success criteria.
feedback.b: Perfect briefing. You've covered all four elements: objective (primary assessment), task (assess the patient), time limit (8 minutes), and success criteria (identify all conditions, call correct resources). Clear and under 60 seconds.
feedback.c: A 10-minute lecture before VR kills momentum and overloads working memory. Students learn by doing, not by listening to theory right before doing. Keep the briefing under 60 seconds.

s3.context: Phase 2: During the VR experience. Students are in the simulation performing patient assessments. You're watching via casting.

choices.a: Step in frequently to correct every small mistake in real time
choices.b: Leave the room — students need to figure it out alone
choices.c: Observe via casting, note strengths and specific errors for debriefing, and only intervene with a brief prompt if a student is clearly stuck for more than a minute

feedback.a: Constant corrections interrupt flow, destroy immersion, and prevent students from learning from their own mistakes. Save your observations for debriefing.
feedback.b: Students need you present — to handle technical issues, notice if someone is stuck, and collect observations for debriefing. Unsupervised VR is risky and misses learning opportunities.
feedback.c: Correct. Observe, take notes, and let students learn from the experience. Intervene only when truly stuck (not just struggling — struggling is learning). Your notes become the basis for effective debriefing.

s4.context: Phase 3: Debriefing. Headsets are off. Students look energised but you need to extract learning from the experience.

choices.a: Ask structured questions: 'Walk me through your first decision. What did you notice about the patient? What would you do differently?' — then connect to real-world practice.
choices.b: Ask 'Did you enjoy it?' and move to the next topic if they say yes.
choices.c: Show the correct procedure on a slideshow and tell them what they should have done.

feedback.a: Correct approach. Structured reflection questions force students to reconstruct their experience, analyse their decisions, and articulate what they learned. Connecting to real practice completes the transfer.
feedback.b: Enjoyment confirms engagement, not learning. Without structured reflection, the experience stays as a memory of 'something cool' rather than a transferable skill. Always follow up with 'What did you decide? Why?'
feedback.c: Lecturing about the correct answer after the experience defeats the purpose of debriefing. Students learn more from reflecting on their own choices than from being told the right answer.

s5.context: Timing: You have a 30-minute slot. How would you allocate the time?

choices.a: 25 minutes VR, 5 minutes debriefing
choices.b: 5 minutes briefing, 15 minutes VR experience, 10 minutes debriefing
choices.c: 15 minutes briefing, 15 minutes VR

feedback.a: 25 minutes in VR is too long — attention drops sharply after 15 minutes. And 5 minutes of debriefing is barely enough. The experience-to-reflection ratio should be closer to 1:1.
feedback.b: Well balanced. Brief and focused briefing (even less time is fine), a solid VR experience within the optimal attention window, and enough debriefing time to extract genuine learning. This is the recommended allocation.
feedback.c: A 15-minute briefing is far too long — you've used half the session on theory before students even start. Keep briefing under 5 minutes and give that time to the experience and debriefing.

s6.context: Your session plan is complete: briefing → experience → debriefing, all within 30 minutes.
s6.confirm: I've planned a complete three-phase VR session with proper timing, clear briefing structure, appropriate instructor behaviour during VR, and structured debriefing questions.

activitySelection.title: Activity Selection
activitySelection.description: Choose the right VR activity type for different learning goals.

s1.context: Three teachers need help choosing the right VR activity type for their upcoming lessons.
s1.body: Remember the three types: Guided Exploration (familiarise with an environment), Simulation with Practice (perform tasks and develop skills), and Evaluation / Skill Check (demonstrate competence under assessment conditions).

s2.context: Teacher 1: 'My architecture students have never seen a Gothic cathedral up close. I want them to understand the structural elements before we start the design unit.'

choices.a: Guided Exploration — students walk through the virtual cathedral with focus questions like 'Identify three load-bearing structural elements and explain their function.'
choices.b: Simulation with Practice — students should build a cathedral immediately
choices.c: Evaluation — test what they already know about Gothic architecture

feedback.a: Correct. Students need to familiarise themselves with Gothic structures before designing anything. Guided Exploration with a focus question turns virtual tourism into active observation.
feedback.b: Building is premature — students haven't seen or studied the structures yet. Exploration must precede practice when the environment is new.
feedback.c: You can't evaluate knowledge students don't have yet. Assessment comes after exploration and practice.

s3.context: Teacher 2: 'My nursing students have been studying CPR theory for two weeks. Now they need to practice performing it correctly before their clinical placement next month.'
s3.tip: Match the learning goal (practice a skill) to the right activity type.

choices.a: Guided Exploration — let them walk around a virtual hospital first
choices.b: Simulation with Practice — students perform CPR on a virtual patient, receiving real-time feedback on compression depth, rate, and hand placement.
choices.c: Show them a VR video of someone performing CPR

feedback.a: Students already know the theory — they need practice, not more exploration. Hospital familiarisation could be useful, but the primary need is skill practice.
feedback.b: Correct. After two weeks of theory, students need to apply what they've learned. Simulation with Practice provides the repetition, feedback, and safe failure space that builds competence.
feedback.c: Watching CPR is passive. Students need to PERFORM CPR to develop the skill. VR's value is in the practice, not the viewing.

s4.context: Teacher 3: 'My students have completed five VR practice sessions on equipment operation. I need to verify they can do it independently before they work on the real machines.'

choices.a: More Guided Exploration of the equipment
choices.b: Another round of Simulation with Practice
choices.c: Evaluation / Skill Check — students demonstrate the complete procedure under assessment conditions, with no prompts or guidance, and performance is scored on accuracy, sequence, and timing.

feedback.a: After five practice sessions, students know the equipment. More exploration would be a step backward.
feedback.b: At some point, practice must transition to evaluation. Five sessions is sufficient preparation. The goal now is to confirm competence.
feedback.c: Correct. After sufficient practice, Evaluation / Skill Check confirms that students can perform independently under realistic conditions. This is the final checkpoint before they work on real equipment.

s5.context: You've helped all three teachers choose the right activity type.
s5.confirm: I can match learning goals to VR activity types: Guided Exploration for familiarisation, Simulation with Practice for skill development, and Evaluation / Skill Check for competency validation.

lessonDesign.title: Design a VR Lesson
lessonDesign.description: Apply everything you've learned to design a complete VR learning experience from scratch.

s1.context: You're the VR coordinator at a medical training centre. A new cohort of paramedic students starts next week, and you need to design a complete VR lesson on initial trauma assessment.
s1.body: This boss mission combines all Module 2 concepts: writing objectives, choosing activity types, planning session flow, and ensuring active (not passive) learning. Let's design this lesson step by step.

s2.context: Step 1: Write the learning objective. The topic is initial trauma assessment — students need to be able to assess a patient at an accident scene.

choices.a: Students will understand trauma assessment procedures
choices.b: Students will perform a primary trauma assessment on a virtual accident patient, identifying all life-threatening conditions and initiating correct treatment within 5 minutes.
choices.c: Students will watch a trauma assessment simulation in VR

feedback.a: 'Understand' is passive and unmeasurable. You've fallen into the classic trap verb. What should students DO?
feedback.b: Perfect objective. Action verb (perform), specific context (virtual accident patient), and measurable criteria (all conditions identified, correct treatment, 5-minute limit). This is ready for VR.
feedback.c: 'Watch' is passive observation. Paramedics don't watch — they act. The objective should describe performing the assessment, not viewing it.

s3.context: Step 2: Students have never seen the virtual accident scene before. Which activity type do you use for their first session?

choices.a: Start with Guided Exploration — students walk through the accident scene and identify key elements (patient location, hazards, available equipment) before they perform any assessment.
choices.b: Jump straight to the simulation — they'll figure it out
choices.c: Start with Evaluation — test their baseline knowledge

feedback.a: Correct. New environment = exploration first. Students familiarise themselves with the scene layout, spot hazards, and locate equipment. This spatial awareness pays off when the simulation begins.
feedback.b: In a new, complex environment, students will spend the first few minutes lost and confused instead of focusing on the assessment. A short exploration phase prevents this.
feedback.c: Evaluating students before they've practiced guarantees failure and frustration. Assessment comes after exploration and practice.

s4.context: Step 3: Plan the briefing. You have 60 seconds before students put on headsets for the exploration phase.
s4.tip: Cover: objective, task, time limit, success criteria.

choices.a: Give a 10-minute lecture on trauma assessment theory
choices.b: 'Walk through the accident scene. You have 5 minutes. Find the patient, identify three hazards, and locate the medical kit. When time is up, remove your headsets — we'll discuss what you found.'
choices.c: Just say 'Explore the scene and have fun'

feedback.a: A lecture before VR kills momentum and overloads working memory. The briefing should be a quick, clear statement of what to do — not a theory review.
feedback.b: Excellent briefing. Specific task (find patient, identify hazards, locate kit), clear time limit (5 minutes), and defined success criteria. Under 60 seconds. Students know exactly what to do.
feedback.c: 'Have fun' gives no direction. Students will wander randomly instead of observing systematically. Always give a focus task during exploration.

s5.context: Step 4: After exploration, students move to the main simulation — performing the trauma assessment. How long should the active VR experience last?

choices.a: 30 minutes — longer is better for skill development
choices.b: 5 minutes — keep it very short
choices.c: 10–15 minutes — enough time for a complete assessment attempt while staying within the optimal attention window

feedback.a: 30 minutes in VR causes attention drops, eye strain, and diminishing returns. After 15 minutes, cognitive focus drops sharply. Multiple shorter sessions are more effective than one long one.
feedback.b: 5 minutes may not be enough for a complete trauma assessment. Students need time to work through the full procedure. 10–15 minutes balances depth with attention capacity.
feedback.c: Correct. 10–15 minutes provides enough time for a complete assessment attempt while keeping students in the optimal cognitive engagement window. Shorter sessions can be repeated for practice.

s6.context: Step 5: During the simulation, a student has been standing still for 2 minutes, staring at the patient without acting.

choices.a: Offer a brief, specific prompt: 'Start with the airway — check if the patient is breathing.' Then step back and let them continue.
choices.b: Give detailed instructions for the entire assessment sequence
choices.c: Let them figure it out — struggling is learning

feedback.a: Correct. A minimal prompt unblocks the student without doing the task for them. One specific nudge ('Start with the airway') is enough to get them moving while preserving autonomy.
feedback.b: Giving the full procedure defeats the purpose of simulation. Students need to recall and apply the steps themselves. A small nudge is enough.
feedback.c: Struggling with a challenge is learning. But standing still for 2+ minutes means the student is stuck, not struggling. A brief prompt prevents frustration while keeping the learning experience productive.

s7.context: Step 6: The simulation is over. Headsets are off. Time to debrief. A student says 'That was intense.' Others nod.

choices.a: Say 'Great!' and move to the next topic
choices.b: Show a video of the correct procedure
choices.c: Ask: 'Walk me through your first action at the scene. What did you check first? What did you find? If you were called to a real accident tomorrow, what would you do the same — and what would you change?'

feedback.a: 'Intense' is an emotional response, not learning. Without structured reflection, students remember the feeling but not the lessons. Always follow up with specific questions.
feedback.b: Showing the correct answer turns debriefing into a lecture. Students learn more by reconstructing their own decisions than by being told what they should have done.
feedback.c: Excellent debriefing approach. You start with recall (what did you do), move to analysis (what worked, what didn't), and end with transfer (what would you do in real life). This extracts maximum learning from the experience.

s8.context: Step 7: During debriefing, a student says: 'I panicked when I saw the blood and forgot the assessment sequence completely.'

choices.a: Tell them they failed and need to study more
choices.b: Normalise it: 'That's exactly why we practice in VR — so you can experience that panic in a safe environment. Next time, you'll remember to start with the airway check because you've now felt what it's like to forget.' Schedule another practice session.
choices.c: Avoid discussing the panic — focus only on what went right

feedback.a: Calling it failure discourages the student. VR is a safe practice environment — the whole point is to make mistakes without consequences. Use the mistake as a learning moment.
feedback.b: Perfect response. You've normalised the experience (panic is natural), shown the value of VR practice (safe environment for mistakes), identified the lesson (start with airway), and offered a path forward (another session). This is supportive, constructive debriefing.
feedback.c: The panic IS the learning opportunity. By addressing it directly, you help the student develop strategies for managing stress in real situations. Avoiding it wastes a valuable teachable moment.

s9.context: Step 8: After several practice sessions, you want to confirm students are ready for their clinical placement. Which activity type do you use?

choices.a: Another Guided Exploration of the accident scene
choices.b: Evaluation / Skill Check — students perform the full trauma assessment under exam conditions: no prompts, no guidance, scored on accuracy, sequence, timing, and decision quality.
choices.c: A written theory exam about trauma assessment

feedback.a: Students already know the environment from multiple sessions. More exploration adds nothing at this stage. They need to demonstrate competence.
feedback.b: Correct. After sufficient practice, Evaluation / Skill Check is the appropriate final step. It confirms that students can perform independently under realistic conditions — the last checkpoint before real clinical work.
feedback.c: A written exam tests recall, not performance. Paramedics need to demonstrate the physical and cognitive skills in context. VR assessment is more authentic than a paper test for procedural skills.

faqs.metaDesc: Frequently asked questions about using VR Education Hub and integrating VR into education.
faqs.title: FAQs
faqs.subtitle: Quick answers to common questions. More items will be added as the platform evolves.
faqs.a1: Not yet. The Bootcamp is under development and will be released in stages.
faqs.q2: Where can I find technical requirements for VR hardware?
faqs.a2: Use the Documentation section to browse hardware and setup topics, including recommended specs and troubleshooting.
faqs.q3: Can I switch the site language?
faqs.a3: Yes. Use the language switcher in the top-right corner to change between English and Spanish.
faqs.q4: Who is VR Education Hub for?
faqs.a4: VR Education Hub is aimed at teachers, trainers, and educators at any level who want to introduce or improve the use of virtual reality in their teaching. No prior VR experience is required; the documentation and Bootcamp are designed for beginners and those looking to deepen their practice.
faqs.a5: Yes. The documentation and learning resources on VR Education Hub are free to use. Our goal is to make VR in education accessible to as many educators as possible.
faqs.q6: What age is VR suitable for in the classroom?
faqs.a6: Recommendations vary by headset and app. Many classroom VR solutions are designed for students aged 13 and older, but some experiences are suitable for younger learners with supervision. Check the Documentation section for age guidelines and safety best practices specific to your hardware and use case.
faqs.q7: How do I search the documentation?
faqs.a7: On the Documentation page you can type in the search box to filter modules by text (titles and content). You can also use the Categories filter in the sidebar to narrow results by topic (e.g. VR Fundamentals, Hardware & Setup, Pedagogical Design, Implementation).
faqs.q8: How often is the content updated?
faqs.a8: We update the documentation as new modules are ready and when hardware or best practices change. The Bootcamp will be released in stages. Check back periodically or browse the Documentation for the latest guides and recommendations.
